[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Eight Precepts|ja|八斎戒}} - Saikai in Buddhism * {{ill|Fasting in Islam|ja|イスラム教における斎戒}} * {{ill|Great Lent|ja|大斎 (カトリック教会)}} * {{ill|Imi|ja|忌み}} * {{ill|Yagyō-san|ja|夜行さん}} * {{ill|Mikaribaba|ja|箕借り婆}}
*[[Abe no Seimei]] * [[Da Liu Ren]] * [[Goryō]] * [[Itako]] * [[Kuji-in]] * [[Onmyōdō]] * [[Sanpaku]] * [[Seimei Shrine]] * [[Senji Ryakketsu]] * [[Shikigami]] * [[Shinigami]] * [[Tengenjutsu (fortune telling)]] * [[Category:Religious Studies]] [[Category:Folk Studies]] [[ja:物忌み]] [[Ushi no toki mairi]]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Eight Precepts|ja|八斎戒}} - Saikai in Buddhism * {{ill|Fasting in Islam|ja|イスラム教における斎戒}} * {{ill|Great Lent|ja|大斎 (カトリック教会)}} * {{ill|Imi|ja|忌み}} * {{ill|Yagyō-san|ja|夜行さん}} * {{ill|Mikaribaba|ja|箕借り婆}}

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Eight Precepts|ja|八斎戒}} - Saikai in Buddhism * {{ill|Fasting in Islam|ja|イスラム教における斎戒}} * {{ill|Great Lent|ja|大斎 (カトリック教会)}} * {{ill|Imi|ja|忌み}} * {{ill|Yagyō-san|ja|夜行さん}} * {{ill|Mikaribaba|ja|箕借り婆}}
*[[Abe no Seimei]] * [[Da Liu Ren]] * [[Goryō]] * [[Itako]] * [[Kuji-in]] * [[Onmyōdō]] * [[Sanpaku]] * [[Seimei Shrine]] * [[Senji Ryakketsu]] * [[Shikigami]] * [[Shinigami]] * [[Tengenjutsu (fortune telling)]] * [[Category:Religious Studies]] [[Category:Folk Studies]] [[ja:物忌み]] [[Ushi no toki mairi]]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Eight Precepts|ja|八斎戒}} - Saikai in Buddhism * {{ill|Fasting in Islam|ja|イスラム教における斎戒}} * {{ill|Great Lent|ja|大斎 (カトリック教会)}} * {{ill|Imi|ja|忌み}} * {{ill|Yagyō-san|ja|夜行さん}} * {{ill|Mikaribaba|ja|箕借り婆}}

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Eight Precepts|ja|八斎戒}} - Saikai in Buddhism * {{ill|Fasting in Islam|ja|イスラム教における斎戒}} * {{ill|Great Lent|ja|大斎 (カトリック教会)}} * {{ill|Imi|ja|忌み}} * {{ill|Yagyō-san|ja|夜行さん}} * {{ill|Mikaribaba|ja|箕借り婆}}
*[[Abe no Seimei]] * [[Da Liu Ren]] * [[Goryō]] * [[Itako]] * [[Kuji-in]] * [[Onmyōdō]] * [[Sanpaku]] * [[Seimei Shrine]] * [[Senji Ryakketsu]] * [[Shikigami]] * [[Shinigami]] * [[Tengenjutsu (fortune telling)]] * [[Category:Religious Studies]] [[Category:Folk Studies]] [[ja:物忌み]] [[Ushi no toki mairi]]

It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.
