{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Eight Precepts|ja|八斎戒}} - Saikai in Buddhism * {{ill|Fasting in Islam|ja|イスラム教における斎戒}} * {{ill|Great Lent|ja|大斎 (カトリック教会)}} * {{ill|Imi|ja|忌み}} * {{ill|Yagyō-san|ja|夜行さん}} * {{ill|Mikaribaba|ja|箕借り婆}}
*[[Abe no Seimei]] * [[Da Liu Ren]] * [[Goryō]] * [[Itako]] * [[Kuji-in]] * [[Onmyōdō]] * [[Sanpaku]] * [[Seimei Shrine]] * [[Senji Ryakketsu]] * [[Shikigami]] * [[Shinigami]] * [[Tengenjutsu (fortune telling)]] * [[Category:Religious Studies]] [[Category:Folk Studies]] [[ja:物忌み]] [[Ushi no toki mairi]]

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Monoimi]][[en:draft:Monoimi]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Monoimi|Monoimi]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=contrary to wikidata idk if this deserves an article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:22, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>
Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the {{ill|marebito|ja|まれびと}} (visiting gods).
== Monoimi in the Jingiryō == The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令}} section of the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. For grand ceremonies such as the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.
The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the {{ill|Saigu|ja|斎宮}} of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.
== Modern Regulations == The {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.
== Monoimi in Folk Practices == Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling {{ill|night soil|ja|下肥}} during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.
Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on {{ill|Kōzushima|ja|神津島}}, residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>
Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

revision.text:
'''物忌み''' （ものいみ）とは、ある期間中、ある種の日常的な行為をひかえ[[穢れ]]を避けること。'''斎戒'''に同じ<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor=[[桜井徳太郎]]|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>。
具体的には、肉食や匂いの強い野菜の摂取を避け、他の者と火を共有しないなどの禁止事項がある。日常的な行為をひかえることには、自らの穢れを抑える面と、来訪神 ([[まれびと]])などの神聖な存在に穢れを移さないためという面がある。
==神祇令における物忌み== 公的な祭祀における物忌みについては、[[養老律令]]の[[神祇令]]に定められている。大祭、すなわち[[大嘗祭]]においては散斎（あらいみ）一月、致斎（まいみ）三日、中祭は三日、小祭は当日のみとされている<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref>。ここに於いて散斎とは軽度の物忌み、致斎とは厳重な物忌みのことである。
同じく神祇令に大祭にかかる散斎の期間中は、通常の執務を行ってよいが、死者の弔い、[[仏事]]の[[法要]]を行うこと、病人の訪問、肉食、[[死刑]]の実施、刑罰言い渡しの署名、[[笞刑]]の実施、神事の為の[[雅楽]]以外の音楽の演奏、および穢れに関わることを禁ずる<ref>『[[貞観儀式|儀式]] 践祚大嘗祭儀』、皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300～332、p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref>と定められている。更に伊勢神宮の[[斎宮]]に類似する[[斎宮#斎宮の忌み詞|忌語]]、即ち「死をなおる」と、「病をやすみ」と、「哭くを塩たる」と、「血をあせ」と、「宍を菌」（肉食の禁止に由来）と言い換えることも定める。<ref>『[[貞観儀式|儀式]] 践祚大嘗祭儀』、[[皇學館大学]]神道研究所 p.332</ref> 致斎においては祭祀に関わること以外の一切が禁じられている。
==現代の規定== [[神社本庁]]では、「斎戒に関する規定」として大祭、中祭は当日および前日、小祭は当日斎戒するものと定めている<ref>{{cite|和書|author=[[沼部春友]]|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref>。斎戒中は、「潔斎して身体を清め、衣服を改め、居室を別にし、飲食を慎み、思念、言語、動作を正しくし、汚穢、不浄に触れてはならない」とされている。
==民間における物忌み== 民間においても、同様の作法が行われていた。祭りの関係者は祭りの前一定期間は歌を歌わない、肉食をしない、[[下肥]]を扱わない、などという習慣が行われていた。また、地域により[[キュウリ]]、[[ゴマ]]などの摂取を禁止する例もある。
その他に季節ごとの神の来訪に合わせた物忌みが行われていた。例えば[[神津島]]では旧暦1月24日夜に海から訪れる神を迎えるため、20日ごろから山に入ることを控え、当日は仕事を休み、物音を立てないようにし、夜間は明かりもつけず、戸を開けることもしないという物忌みが現在も行われている<ref>{{cite|和書|author=[[田中宣一]]|contribution=忌み日|editor=[[桜井徳太郎]]|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>。
祭の中心となる[[頭屋]]などには特に長期にわたる物忌みが要求され、やはり散斎と致斎の期間が設けられていた。致斎の期間には特に穢れを防ぐため、精進小屋などで[[別火]]生活が行われた。
==関連項目== *[[八斎戒]] - 仏教における斎戒 *[[イスラム教における斎戒]] *[[大斎 (カトリック教会)]] *[[忌み]] *[[夜行さん]] *[[箕借り婆]]
==脚注== {{Reflist}}
{{Reli-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:ものいみ}} [[Category:宗教学]] [[Category:民俗学]]

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|qq=|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|qq=|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|qq=|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|qq=|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|qq=|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|qq=|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|qq=|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|qq=|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

== Related Articles ==

* {{ill|Imi|ja|忌み|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imi|qq=|1=Abstinence rituals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5857008}}
* {{ill|Yagyō-san|ja|夜行さん|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagyō-san|qq=|1=Yagyō-san|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11431317}}

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|qq=|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|qq=|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|qq=|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|qq=|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|qq=|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|qq=|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|qq=|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|qq=|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

In the future it will be good to import the revisions. '''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.
