'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.

'''Monoimi''' (物忌み) refers to the practice of refraining from certain daily activities for a specific period to avoid defilement (穢れ). It is synonymous with "saikai" (斎戒).<ref>{{cite|和書|author=石川純一郎|contribution=物忌み|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=289}}</ref>

Specific prohibitions include abstaining from consuming meat and strong-smelling vegetables, as well as avoiding shared use of fire with others. The purpose of these practices is twofold: to suppress personal defilement and to prevent transferring defilement to sacred entities such as the [[:en:Marebito|marebito]] (visiting gods).

== Monoimi in the Jingiryō ==
The practice of monoimi in official rituals is specified in the {{ill|Jingiryō|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingiryō|qq=}} section of the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. For grand ceremonies such as the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]], it stipulates a month of "sansai" (散斎) and three days of "chisai" (致斎). For middle ceremonies, three days are required, while minor ceremonies necessitate monoimi only on the day of the ritual.<ref>{{cite web|url={{NDLDC|815509}}|title=神祇令義解講義|accessdate=2014-07-27}}</ref> Here, "sansai" refers to a mild form of abstention, while "chisai" entails stricter observance.

The same Jingiryō states that during the sansai period for grand ceremonies, one may carry out regular duties but must refrain from activities such as funerals, {{ill|Buddhist rites|ja|仏事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist rites|1=Buddhist rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45265996}} like memorial services, visiting the sick, consuming meat, conducting executions, signing sentencing documents, carrying out corporal punishment, playing non-ritual music, and engaging in defiling activities.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.300–332, p.765 ISBN 978-4-7842-1619-2</ref> Additionally, it mandates euphemistic language similar to that used by the [[:en:Saikū|Saigu]] of the Ise Grand Shrine, such as replacing words associated with death, sickness, crying, blood, and meat with alternative terms.<ref>『{{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634544}} 踐祚大嘗祭儀』 皇學館大学神道研究所 p.332</ref> During chisai, all non-ritual activities are strictly prohibited.

== Modern Regulations ==
The [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] specifies monoimi periods in its regulations on "Saikai." For grand and middle ceremonies, monoimi is observed on the day of the ritual and the preceding day, while for minor ceremonies, it is observed on the day of the ritual only.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Numabe Harutomo|ja|沼部春友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numabe Harutomo|1=Harutomo Numabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554635}}|author2=茂木貞純|title=新神社祭式行事作法教本|publisher=戎光祥出版|year=2011|page=280}}</ref> During monoimi, participants are required to purify their bodies, change clothing, live separately, abstain from certain foods and drinks, and maintain purity in thoughts, speech, and actions, avoiding any contact with impurity or uncleanliness.

== Monoimi in Folk Practices ==
Similar customs have been observed in folk practices. Festival participants were traditionally required to refrain from singing, consuming meat, or handling [[:en:Night soil|night soil]] during the festival period. Certain regions also prohibited the consumption of specific foods, such as cucumbers or sesame.

Seasonal monoimi was also practiced to prepare for the arrival of deities. For example, on [[:en:Kōzu-shima|Kōzushima]], residents abstain from entering the mountains, cease work, avoid noise, and extinguish lights on the night of January 24th (lunar calendar) to welcome a sea deity.<ref>{{cite|和書|author={{ill|Tanaka Norikazu|ja|田中宣一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Norikazu|1=Sen'ichi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17160840}}|contribution=忌み日|editor={{ill|Sakurai Tokutarō|ja|桜井徳太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Tokutarō|1=Tokutarō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11537812}}|title=民間信仰辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=1980|page=31}}</ref>

Key festival figures, such as {{ill|Tōya|ja|頭屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōya|1=touya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11664931}}, were often subject to extended periods of monoimi. These included designated periods for sansai and chisai, with the strictest periods requiring them to reside in separate firehouses (separate hearths) to avoid defilement.
